entity: Working in the Time of COVID-19 Oral History Project
summary: I participated in the Working in the Time of COVID-19 Oral History Project on December 18, 2020. This project is important because it captures the experiences of workers like me during the COVID-19 pandemic. It was conducted by Conor Casey from my apartment in Seattle, and I was happy to share my story as a healthcare worker on the frontlines.

The Labor Archives of Washington is the organization behind the oral history project I participated in. They focus on preserving the stories of workers, especially during challenging times like the COVID-19 pandemic. It was through their initiative that I was interviewed about my experiences as a nurse and union delegate during this crisis.

I currently live in Seattle, Washington, specifically on North Beacon Hill. I've worked at Harborview Medical Center here since 2007, and this city has been the center of my professional and personal life for many years. Seattle has been a hub for healthcare during the COVID-19 pandemic, and my work as a nurse has been deeply connected to this community.

I was born in Korea on May 24, 1985, but I was adopted as a baby and raised in Montana. I didn't engage much with my Korean heritage growing up because my adoptive parents are Caucasian. However, I reconnected with my roots during college when I took an Asian history class and even traveled back to Korea. While Korean culture is part of my identity, I mostly identify with being an adoptee and have found a community with other Korean adoptees.

Although I was born in Korea, I grew up in Montana after being adopted as an infant. Montana was where I was raised by my Caucasian adoptive parents and where my early life experiences took place before moving to Seattle. It's part of my background but not where I built my professional career.

I grew up attending church and went to a Catholic college, so I have respect for the Catholic Church and religion in general. However, I don't personally identify with any strong religious beliefs and consider myself more of a gnostic. My connection to the Catholic Church is more from my upbringing and education rather than personal faith.

I have worked at Harborview Medical Center in Seattle since 2007, starting on a trauma surgical acute care unit and later moving to trauma surgical intensive care. Harborview is the only level one trauma center in the four-state area including Alaska, Idaho, and Montana. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Harborview converted units into COVID ICUs where I have been working extensively as an ECMO specialist and nurse.

I am a registered nurse and have been since I got my license in 2007. My entire nursing career has been at Harborview Medical Center, where I specialize in trauma surgical intensive care and ECMO. Being a nurse means working long shifts, often nights, and providing critical care to patients, including those severely affected by COVID-19.

As an ECMO specialist, I operate a machine that provides advanced life support by bypassing the lungs or heart. This therapy has become especially important during the COVID-19 pandemic because it helps critically ill patients who need lung or heart support. I spend entire 12-hour shifts monitoring the ECMO machine while caring for patients in the COVID ICU at Harborview.

I am a member and union delegate for SEIU 1199 at Harborview Medical Center. Although I was a member since starting in 2007, I became more active around 2019 when I participated in contract bargaining. During the pandemic, I have served as interim co-chair for the nurse staffing committee and chaired joint labor management meetings, helping negotiate issues like PPE, furloughs, and redeployment.

Working in the trauma surgical ICU in Seattle, I see many patients from the Pacific Northwest region. This area is very active, with lots of outdoor accidents, motor vehicle crashes, and penetrating traumas like gunshot wounds. Harborview serves as the only level one trauma center in this region, so we handle a wide range of emergencies from across the Pacific Northwest.

Harborview Medical Center serves as the only level one trauma center for a four-state area that includes Alaska. This means I sometimes care for patients who have been transferred from Alaska for critical trauma and intensive care. Alaska is part of the wider regional community that relies on Harborview for advanced medical services.

Along with Alaska, Montana, and Washington, Harborview Medical Center serves patients from Idaho as the only level one trauma center in the region. I have cared for trauma patients coming from Idaho who require intensive surgical and critical care. This regional responsibility shapes much of the work I do as a nurse in Seattle.

I took an Asian history class at the University of Washington during college, which helped me reconnect with my Korean heritage. Although I did not grow up with a strong ethnic identity, my time at UW was important for exploring that part of myself. The university experience also coincided with my growing awareness of my identity as a Korean adoptee.

The COVID-19 pandemic has drastically changed my work as a nurse at Harborview. Since early 2020, I have been working mostly in newly created COVID ICUs, caring for critically ill patients and managing ECMO therapy. The pandemic has introduced new challenges like extensive PPE use, frequent bargaining over safety protocols, and the mental toll of prolonged crisis care.

I was designated a tier one frontline healthcare worker and received the COVID-19 vaccine in December 2020. Getting vaccinated was a moment of relief and hope during a bleak time, and I felt fortunate to qualify early because of my frequent contact with COVID-positive patients. The vaccine has helped reduce some of the fear around my health risks at work.

The term 'Chinese virus' was mentioned in the context of the pandemic, but I focus on the medical and community aspects of COVID-19 rather than politicized language. My experience centers on caring for patients and protecting myself and others through proper PPE and vaccination. The pandemic's impact on healthcare workers like me transcends such terminology.
